title: Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census – updated March 2020 | Stats NZ
content:
## Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census – updated March 2020
12 March 2020, 11:30am
Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census contains counts at statistical area 1 (SA1) and other geographic levels for selected variables from the 2018, 2013, and 2006 censuses. The geographies correspond to 2018 boundaries.
4 February 2022 : In November 2021 we amended two footnotes in the dwelling tables in each of the following Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census files:
- all regional Excel workbooks
- Total New Zealand Excel workbook .
See Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census – updates and corrections for further information on updates and corrections.
This dataset has been designed to provide data for small geographic areas with:
- less suppression for single variable data
- more detailed levels of classifications for some variables
- simple cross-tabulations, for example, age in five-year groups by sex.
The statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census replaces the meshblock dataset released in previous censuses. Meshblocks remain the smallest geographic unit but are no longer a standard output geography. Data based on non-standard geographies (including meshblocks) is available by emailing info@stats.govt.nz . See Customised data services for further information.
The statistical area 1 dataset for the 2018 Census is available in Excel (.xlsx), CSV long format (vertical), and CSV wide format (horizontal) files, and can be integrated with analytical or mapping systems (using geographic boundary files), enabling further analysis of census data.
Note: Key variables from the statistical area 1 dataset are available to download in a variety of spatial formats from the Stats NZ Geographic Data Service ( Datafinder ).

### Total New Zealand files
Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census, total New Zealand (updated November 2021) – Microsoft Excel Open XML (zipped file, 237MB) Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census, total New Zealand (updated 16 July 2020) – CSV (zipped file, 32MB) Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census, total New Zealand – long format (updated 16 July 2020) – CSV (zipped file, 214MB)
Note: Files may take a while to download.
See Statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census – updates and corrections for corrections to these datasets since the original release on 16 December 2019.

### Regional files (Microsoft Excel Open XML)
Northland region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 9MB) Auckland region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 74MB) Waikato region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 23MB) Bay of Plenty region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 15MB) Gisborne region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 3MB) Hawke’s Bay region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 8MB) Taranaki region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 6MB) Manawatū-Whanganui region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 13MB) Wellington region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 26MB) Tasman region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 3MB) Nelson region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 3MB) Marlborough region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 3MB) West Coast region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 2MB) Canterbury region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 31MB) Otago region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 12MB) Southland region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 6MB) Area outside region (updated November 2021) (zipped file, 491KB)

All files are zipped for downloading. They are not suitable for printing. We will periodically review how we provide this data.

### Viewing the long format CSV files
Long-format CSV is a new format for the statistical area 1 dataset. The zipped file contains six folders for the topic areas. Each folder contains individual CSV files for each variable. The long-format CSV files contain:
- one column for all geography codes
- one column for all geography descriptions
- a variable code column
- a variable description column.

### Viewing the wide format CSV files and Excel workbooks
The zipped file contains six files relating to the variable topics.
Auckland local board areas are listed at the end of the statistical area 1 dataset. The sum of these areas is already included in the total New Zealand figures.
The SA1 geography does not have a description field – it is a code-only classification.
Some geographies have codes that contain leading zeros. Opening the CSV file in Excel automatically suppresses leading zeros.
You may wish to replace some characters with a numerical value to help you process the data. These characters are:
- confidentialised cells, represented by ‘C’
- figure not available, represented by ‘..’.
Footnotes and symbols are the same as in the Excel files, but commas and hyperlinks have been removed. Footnotes for the CSVs are given in separate PDF files.

## Geographies available
The statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census consists of a set of Excel workbooks or CSV files for total New Zealand. There is also a set of six Excel workbooks for each regional council area. The workbook or file contains the following geographies:
- district health board
- local board area (Auckland region only)
- territorial authority
Statistical area 1 (SA1) and statistical area 2 (SA2) geographies are aggregations of meshblocks optimised to be of similar population sizes to enable the release of low-level data. They are non-administrative areas that are in-between meshblocks and territorial authorities in size. Statistical areas either define or aggregate to define urban rural areas, territorial authorities, and regional councils. The statistical geographies are defined by the Statistical standard for geographic areas 2018 , which replaced the New Zealand Standard Areas Classification 1992.
The SA1 geography is a new output geography for the 2018 Census that provides more detailed information about population characteristics than is available at the meshblock level. They are derived from one or more meshblocks and are intended to be the smallest geographic area for published output. SA1s have an ideal size range of 100–200 residents, and a maximum population of about 500. Around 19,000 of the 29,889 SA1s (about two-thirds) consist of a single meshblock.
The SA2 geography replaces the area unit geography. Although SA2s and area units are generally the same conceptually, the boundaries (and their names) have changed to reflect changes in land use and population patterns since the area unit geography was created in 1992. For further information refer to the Statistical standard for geographic areas 2018 .
Wards are the voting districts for territorial authorities and were first established in November 1989. They are defined under the Local Electoral Act 2001 and boundaries must coincide with meshblock boundaries under section 19T of the Act . The ward system was designed to allow communities within a territorial authority to be recognised, and to increase community involvement in local government.
District health boards are administered under the New Zealand Public Health and Disability Act 2000. Boundaries represent geographic areas based on territorial authority and ward boundaries as constituted at 1 January 2001.
Local boards were introduced as part of the new local government arrangements for Auckland in 2010. Local board boundaries must coincide with meshblock boundaries. Local boards share governance with a council’s governing body, and each has complementary responsibilities guaranteed by legislation. Local boards can propose bylaws and they gather community views on local and regional matters.
Note: Ward, local board areas, and district health boards do not align to statistical geographies – SA1, SA2, and urban rural. For further information, please refer to Geographic hierarchy .
In each set of workbooks or files, the variables are divided as follows.
- Census usually resident population count
- Census night population count
- Unit record data source
- Age in five-year groups, including median age
- Age in five-year groups by sex, including median age
- Years at usual residence
- Usual residence five years ago (2013) indicator
- Usual residence one year ago (2017) indicator
- Birthplace (broad geographic areas) excluding at SA1
- Years since arrival in New Zealand, for the overseas-born
- Ethnic group (grouped total responses)
- Six languages (total responses)
- Religious affiliation (total responses)
- Cigarette smoking behaviour
- Difficulty seeing even if wearing glasses
- Difficulty hearing even if using a hearing aid
- Difficulty walking or climbing steps
- Difficulty remembering or concentrating
- Difficulty washing all over or dressing
- Difficulty communicating using your usual language for example being understood by others
- Legally registered relationship status
- Partnership status in current relationship
- Individual home ownership
- Number of children born
- Highest qualification
- Total personal income (grouped), including median total personal income
- Sources of personal income (total responses)
- Main means of travel to education, by usual residence address (2018 only)
- Main means of travel to education, by educational institution address (2018 only)

### Individual (part 3a)
- Work and labour force status
- Status in employment
- Occupation – major group, by usual residence address
- Occupation – major group, by workplace address*
- Industry (division), by usual residence address
- Industry (division), by workplace address*

### Individual (part 3b)
- Total hours worked in employment per week
- Main means of travel to work, by usual residence address
- Main means of travel to work, by workplace address*
- Occupied private dwelling type
- Dwelling record type, for occupied dwellings
- Occupied non-private dwelling type
- Main types of heating used to heat dwellings (total responses)
- Fuel types used to heat dwelling (total responses)
- Dwelling occupancy status
- Access to basic amenities
- Dwelling dampness indicator
- Dwelling mould indicator
- Weekly rent paid by household, including median weekly rent paid by household
- Number of motor vehicles
- Access to telecommunication systems (total responses)
* Workplace address is coded from information supplied by respondents about their workplaces. Where respondents do not supply sufficient information, their responses are coded to ‘not further defined’. The statistical area 1 dataset for 2018 Census excludes these ‘not further defined’ areas.
The dataset uses the most recent version of the variable classifications available at the time of the 2018 Census.

## Geographic boundaries and maps
Geographic data service provides an easy-to-use platform to search, browse, and download geographic boundaries in a variety of formats. SA1s, SA2s, wards, district health boards, local board areas (which sit within the community board classification) territorial authorities, and regional councils are available to view and download.
Geographic boundary viewer lets you view and compare geographic boundaries for a range of years.
Examples of geographic boundary layers has explanatory information about the geographic areas used by Stats NZ.
The 2018 Census confidentiality rules have been applied to 2006, 2013, and 2018 data. These rules protect the confidentiality of individuals, families, households, dwellings, and undertakings in 2018 Census data.
Counts are calculated using fixed random rounding to base 3 (FRR3), and suppression of ‘sensitive’ counts less than six, where tables report multiple geographic variables and/or small populations. Individual figures may not always sum to stated totals.
Medians are calculated from unrounded counts, with input noise added to or subtracted from each contributing value during measures calculation. Medians based on less than six individuals are suppressed.
For more information on the most recent 2018 Census confidentiality rules, see Applying confidentiality rules to 2018 Census data and summary of changes since 2013 .

## About the 2018 Census dataset
We combined data from the census forms with administrative data to create the 2018 Census dataset, which meets Stats NZ’s quality criteria for population structure information.
We added real data about real people to the dataset where we were confident the people should be counted but hadn’t completed a census form. We also used data from the 2013 Census and administrative sources and statistical imputation methods to fill in some missing characteristics of people and dwellings.
An independent panel of experts has assessed the quality of the 2018 Census dataset. The panel has endorsed Stats NZ’s overall methods and concluded that the use of government administrative records has improved the coverage of key variables such as age, sex, ethnicity, and place. The panel’s Initial report of the 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel (September 2019) assessed the methodologies used by Stats NZ to produce the final dataset, as well as the quality of some of the key variables.
Its second report, 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel: Assessment of variables (December 2019) assessed an additional 31 variables.
In its third report, Final report of the 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel (February 2020), the panel made 24 recommendations, several relating to preparations for the 2023 Census. Along with this report, the panel, supported by Stats NZ, produced a series of graphs summarising the sources of data for key 2018 Census individual variables, 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel: Data sources for key 2018 Census individual variables .
2018 Census data user guide provides information about collecting, processing, interpreting, and ways of using 2018 Census data.
We evaluated each variable and assigned them an overall quality rating on a five-point scale ranging from very high to very poor, using a similar rating scale as was used in the 2013 Census. This quality rating provides an overall evaluation of 2018 Census data quality for that single variable.
Data quality assurance for 2018 Census has more detail on the process.
There is variability in the quality of data at smaller geographies and we have identified variables in the statistical area 1 dataset where more caution is required. Please refer to the footnotes in relevant tables for this information.
Data quality ratings for 2018 Census variables lists the ratings used to describe the quality of variables output from the 2018 Census.
2018 Census information by variable and quality provides detailed information about each variable, including definition, priority level in the quality management strategy, quality rating, subject population, question format, data sources, classification information, missing and residual response information, comparability with previous census data, data use, and any issues to be aware of relating to the variable.
Shona Devine 04 931 4600 info@stats.govt.nz
